trans-unit.source: 🚧 This section of the training platform and the entire content is under ⚠️construction⚠️ and may not be shared or published! 🚧
trans-unit.target: 🚧 This section of the training platform and the entire content is under ⚠️construction⚠️ and may not be shared or published! 🚧

trans-unit.source: For Camp 18 in Kutupalong Refugee Camp, we have a dataset on the locations of water distribution points. Further we have data on the buildings (footprint and centroid) within camp 18. In the following task we will use the following dataset:
trans-unit.target: For Camp 18 in Kutupalong Refugee Camp, we have a dataset on the locations of water distribution points. Further we have data on the buildings (footprint and centroid) within camp 18. In the following task we will use the following dataset:

trans-unit.source: Download all datasets __[here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/Modul_9/Modul_9_Exercise_1_Assess_water_distribution_access/Modul_9_Exercise_1.zip)__. Save the folder on your computer. Unzip the .zip file. The unzipped folder is structured according to the recommended folder structure for QGIS projects. Under "data > input" you find the geopackage file `task1.gpkg` which contains the following vector layers:
trans-unit.target: Download all datasets __[here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/Modul_9/Modul_9_Exercise_1_Assess_water_distribution_access/Modul_9_Exercise_1.zip)__. Save the folder on your computer. Unzip the .zip file. The unzipped folder is structured according to the recommended folder structure for QGIS projects. Under "data > input" you find the geopackage file `task1.gpkg` which contains the following vector layers:

trans-unit.source: `camp18_buildings_centroid` (points): Centroids of all building objects inside camp 18
trans-unit.target: `camp18_buildings_centroid` (points): Centroids of all building objects inside camp 18

trans-unit.source: Make sure you installed the ORS Tools plugin and set it up already with an API Key. If not - see the how to here {ref}`content:references:module9:ors-tools-plugin`
trans-unit.target: Make sure you installed the ORS Tools plugin and set it up already with an API Key. If not - see the how to here {ref}`content:references:module9:ors-tools-plugin`

trans-unit.source: Click in the toolbar on the ORS Tools plugin Icon [#$sd1_sf5_dp1]. Click on `Batch Jobs` -> `Isochrones from Layer`. Leave all settings at default except:
trans-unit.target: Click in the toolbar on the ORS Tools plugin Icon [#$sd1_sf5_dp1]. Click on `Batch Jobs` -> `Isochrones from Layer`. Leave all settings at default except:

trans-unit.source: The result layer shows for every waterpoint a 500 meter catchment area for pedestrian travel mode. 500 meter is the maximum distance from any household to the water point according to [The Sphere Handbook: Humanitarian Charter and Minimum Standards in Humanitarian Response](https://spherestandards.org/wp-content/uploads/Sphere-Handbook-2018-EN.pdf) p. 106. There are other key indicators like maximum number of people using the facility, queuing time, etc. We will work on the distance as we miss further information like the flow rate per water point or the household size per building.
trans-unit.target: The result layer shows for every waterpoint a 500 meter catchment area for pedestrian travel mode. 500 meter is the maximum distance from any household to the water point according to [The Sphere Handbook: Humanitarian Charter and Minimum Standards in Humanitarian Response](https://spherestandards.org/wp-content/uploads/Sphere-Handbook-2018-EN.pdf) p. 106. There are other key indicators like maximum number of people using the facility, queuing time, etc. We will work on the distance as we miss further information like the flow rate per water point or the household size per building.

trans-unit.source: We proceed to find out how many water point options are there for each building given the 500m constraint. Basically we add a count aggregation of isochrones that intersect with each building.
trans-unit.target: We proceed to find out how many water point options are there for each building given the 500m constraint. Basically we add a count aggregation of isochrones that intersect with each building.

trans-unit.source: Open the `Processing Toolbox` and scroll down to `Vector general` choose `Join attributes by location (summary)` or enter "Join attributes by location (summary)" in the search bar. Leave all settings at default except:
trans-unit.target: Open the `Processing Toolbox` and scroll down to `Vector general` choose `Join attributes by location (summary)` or enter "Join attributes by location (summary)" in the search bar. Leave all settings at default except:

trans-unit.source: Look into the attributes of the waterpoints layer. Filter for all waterpoints that are operational (search for `Extract by Attribute`). Redo the analysis for operational waterpoints only.
trans-unit.target: Look into the attributes of the waterpoints layer. Filter for all waterpoints that are operational (search for `Extract by Attribute`). Redo the analysis for operational waterpoints only.

trans-unit.source: STEP 3: Waterpoint catchment by closest euclidean distance
trans-unit.target: STEP 3: Waterpoint catchment by closest euclidean distance

trans-unit.source: There are other means to generate catchment areas, implemented in QGIS. In the next part we will use the hub distance to calculate the euclidean distance between every building and its closest waterpoint.
trans-unit.target: There are other means to generate catchment areas, implemented in QGIS. In the next part we will use the hub distance to calculate the euclidean distance between every building and its closest waterpoint.

trans-unit.source: Open the `Processing Toolbox`, choose `Vector Analysis`,then choose `Distance to nearest hub (points)`or enter "Distance to nearest hub (points)" in the search bar. Leave all settings at default except:
trans-unit.target: Open the `Processing Toolbox`, choose `Vector Analysis`,then choose `Distance to nearest hub (points)`or enter "Distance to nearest hub (points)" in the search bar. Leave all settings at default except:

trans-unit.source: The output layer has the same point geometry type like the input building_centroid layer. But it now features two attributes:
trans-unit.target: The output layer has the same point geometry type like the input building_centroid layer. But it now features two attributes:

trans-unit.source: HubName: The name/id of the closest waterpoint.
trans-unit.target: HubName: The name/id of the closest waterpoint.

trans-unit.source: HubDist: The euclidean distance from the building centroid to the closest waterpoint in meters.
trans-unit.target: HubDist: The euclidean distance from the building centroid to the closest waterpoint in meters.

trans-unit.source: All buildings are assigned to their closest waterpoint. With the minimum bounding geometry tool in QGIS we can use this grouping information to create areas.
trans-unit.target: All buildings are assigned to their closest waterpoint. With the minimum bounding geometry tool in QGIS we can use this grouping information to create areas.

trans-unit.source: Open the `Processing Toolbox`, choose `Vector Analysis`, then choose `Minimum Bounding Geometry` or enter "Minimum Bounding Geometry" in the search bar. Leave all settings at default except:
trans-unit.target: Open the `Processing Toolbox`, choose `Vector Analysis`, then choose `Minimum Bounding Geometry` or enter "Minimum Bounding Geometry" in the search bar. Leave all settings at default except:
